slides.title: Your lungs are *quietly aging*. You can slow it.
slides.sub: Elastic recoil fades and lung function drifts down after 30. The right habits keep your breath full for decades.

slides.title: Lung power peaks early
slides.body: Lung function tends to peak in your twenties, then *gently declines* each year as the tissue loses some of its springy elastic recoil.

slides.title: FEV1 drifts downward
slides.body: FEV1, the air you can blow out in one second, falls a little every year after 30. Most people *never notice* until it adds up.

slides.title: Vital capacity is reserve
slides.body: A larger vital capacity is your buffer. More reserve *is linked to* climbing stairs, hiking and laughing without feeling winded later in life.

slides.title: Train the diaphragm
slides.body: The diaphragm is a muscle, so it responds to work. Slow nasal breathing and cardio *can support* stronger, more efficient breaths.

slides.title: Movement keeps lungs young
slides.body: Regular brisk activity keeps the chest and airways supple. Staying active *may help* hold your vital capacity higher for years.

slides.title: Keep your breath *full* for life.
slides.body: Vibe Health tracks the movement, steps, sleep and food behind lasting lung health, then turns them into one daily Health Score.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
Your lungs start aging in your thirties, but breathless old age is not a given. 🌿💨

Elastic recoil and FEV1 slowly decline, yet training your diaphragm and staying active can keep your vital capacity high. Save this. 🔖

Vibe Health turns daily movement and recovery into one simple score → link in bio.
youtube_title: How to Keep Your Lungs Young After 30
